title: Where Am I
html:
A lot of people say heartbreak is the most painful thing you can feel. Can't fix it with a few pills. I get it now. Because where the fuck am I.

A few minutes ago I was at work. Saturday evening shift, the kind that runs long and pays nothing extra. I was watching a woman sob into her wine about her husband — genuinely compelling, the way disasters you're not part of tend to be. She had the look of someone who had held herself together for a long time and run out of reasons to keep doing it. Her mascara was a lost cause. Her husband was apparently also a lost cause. Two for two.

I've never been close enough to anyone to feel what she was feeling. That's not something I'm proud of or ashamed of. It's just the shape of things. It made her easier to watch — no secondary pain, no borrowed grief. I gave her a refill and left her to it.

Then the restaurant disappeared.

No warning. No sound. One second I'm holding a wine bottle and the next I'm holding nothing and standing in a sealed box with about a hundred strangers.

Some of them are shouting. A few are already crying. The air is getting heavy with too many people in too little space. The walls are solid on all four sides — I've checked all four, twice. There's no door. No visible seam that would become a door. The ceiling is around 3 meters up, which helps somewhat. The floor is flat stone or something that reads as stone.

I think: am I being kidnapped.

Then I think: kidnapping usually involves a destination in mind. This feels more like a holding pattern.

I do what I always do when a situation goes bad. I stand still and I look.

The room is roughly the size of a lecture hall. Holding maybe twice as many people as it was built to hold comfortably, and no one here is being comfortable. The smell is already bad — sweat and fear, which are the same smell when they're this concentrated. The crying woman from the restaurant is here. She's found a wall and a fixed point somewhere above the far crowd to stare at. The blank that comes after the breakdown is finished. A man in a suit is on his phone getting nothing — no signal, or nothing to call. Two teenage boys are close to the wall to my right, shoulder to shoulder, not talking. A woman with a baby is sitting on the floor in the corner farthest from the crowd, the baby quiet for now.

I count the people with their backs to walls: twelve. People with their backs to walls are either calculating or exhausted. Everyone in the center of the room with their voice up is burning through oxygen and accomplishing nothing. I note the twelve. I look at their faces. Maybe four of them have the look of someone thinking rather than reacting — eyes moving instead of fixed, weight forward, checking the room the way I'm checking the room.

The oxygen is going to be a real problem in a few hours if nothing changes. Room this size, this many people: somewhere between three and five hours before it gets uncomfortable. That's the most immediate constraint. Everything else is secondary until it isn't.

"Uncle, do you know where we are? I'm so confused."

I look down. A girl has appeared beside me. Young — maybe 16, small, wearing the kind of clothes that suggest she was somewhere normal a few minutes ago, school bag still on one shoulder. She's looking up at me with the focused attention of someone who has identified me as a useful person and is checking whether that assessment holds.

Uncle. The word is a cultural reflex, not a claim. I've seen it before.

"I don't know either," I answer with my best professional smile.

The professional smile is a tool I've been using since I was 18. It communicates calm and competence without promising anything. It works on people who are afraid because what they're looking for is someone who is not. I am not, specifically, afraid. I am recalibrating. The difference is not visible from the outside if you do it right.

I ask her name. She tells me: Soo-Yun. She asks why I'm not joining the protest group building in the center of the room, where the loudest man has started his second lap of screaming at the ceiling.

"There's no door in this room," I say. "Yelling doesn't change that."

She accepts this as a complete answer. I file that. A 16-year-old who accepts a logical statement without needing to argue with it is either unusually sensible or has already done the calculation herself and is just checking her conclusion against mine.

I give her a candy from my pocket. She says her parents told her not to take things from strangers. She takes it anyway and pockets it fast when I tell her not to let anyone else see it. Sensible. I tell her not to call me uncle. She says Sir Eric. I'm not going to win that one.

The protest organizes itself around the loudest man the way protests in enclosed spaces tend to — by volume rather than logic. About half the room joins in phases, each one needing the permission of the one before it. The other half redistributes toward the walls. The twelve become fifteen. The baby in the corner stays quiet, which is either lucky or the baby has also done the math.

---

【"Greetings, Human!"】

【"Welcome to the Tutorial!"】

---

The voice fills the room from no visible source. Womanly. Calm. Coming from everywhere at once, which means it's not coming from any specific place, which means whoever is producing it has infrastructure. One second of total silence follows — the kind that comes when a hundred people all stop talking at exactly the same moment — and then the protest redirects.

---

【"Please prepare yourself!"】

【"Tutorial Chapter 1 will start in 2 hours, enjoy!"】

---

"FUCK! IS THIS A PRANK!?"

The loudest man screams at the ceiling. About half the room joins him like they needed the cue. The other half goes quieter. I note the loudest man's position relative to the walls and relative to Soo-Yun and relative to me. He's burning through oxygen at a rate that's going to tire him out before the two-hour mark. That's fine.

I find a section of wall with a clear line of sight to the main group and put my back to it. Soo-Yun sits down on the floor beside me without asking. I don't tell her to move.

The protest peaks around 30 minutes in and starts declining. That's faster than I expected. Either the room is pulling the energy out of people faster than normal — the oxygen, the stress — or the group has a shorter attention span than average. The crying woman from the restaurant crosses toward a small cluster of wall-sitters around the 30-minute mark. She doesn't say anything immediately. She just stands near them and watches the protest. One of the wall-sitters glances at her. She nods. He nods back. That's a conversation. I note it.

Soo-Yun falls asleep against my shoulder sometime around 40 minutes. I stay awake. I check the walls again more carefully once the noise drops and the crowd thins from the center. The material isn't stone — it's stone-adjacent, slightly warmer than it should be, which suggests insulation or a thin outer layer over something else. No seams lining up with a door anywhere I can reach. The ceiling has a faint texture that wasn't visible in the noise and motion. Tiny perforations, too small to do anything through, the kind you'd put in a surface if you needed airflow without making it obvious.

Someone built this room for extended occupancy. They filled it without telling anyone.

I've been alone my whole adult life. Not in a way I identified as a problem at the time. Some choices close off paths. The path that leads to people depending on you, and you on them — I closed that one early, not deliberately but by repetition. I don't regret it. What I think about sometimes is what it would have cost: the crying woman had loved someone hard enough to be destroyed about losing him. That level of feeling requires an investment I never made. I'm 26 and I have no one to be devastated about.

Not the moment. I set it aside.

The protest is done by 90 minutes in. Not because anything changed — because you can only sustain that volume for so long before your body bills you for it. The room goes quieter. The loudest man ends up near the wall, sitting on the floor, out of energy. He looks younger without the volume. Maybe 24. He catches me looking and doesn't hold it.

"Uncle, are you awake?"

Soo-Yun's face is about 20 centimeters from mine when I open my eyes.

"How long?"

"About an hour, sir Eric."

Still alive. The air tastes about the same as before. Whatever is handling ventilation held up. The crying woman is up from the wall now — Maya, maybe, I'll find out — moving slowly around the edge of the room, looking at the walls the way I did. Eyes still raw from earlier. She's made a decision about something. I can tell by the way she's moving. People move differently once they've decided.

---

【"Tutorial Chapter 01 Starting In 1 Minute!"】

---

About time.

"Kid. Stay close."

I stand and roll my shoulders. The room shifts as everyone rises. The energy redistributes: the protest people go quiet, the quiet ones go alert. Fear is conservative — it stores itself and releases when the trigger arrives. The trigger is arriving.

---

【"3!"】

【"2!"】

---

'Slightly nervous.'

---

【"1!"】

---

'Life is an adventure, I guess.'

---

【"Welcome to Tutorial Chapter 1, enjoy!"】

---

The walls go transparent all at once. Glass-clear. No warning, no gradual fade — solid and then not. On the other side is forest: tall trees, dense undergrowth, the kind of quiet that registers in your chest before you've identified the absence of sound. I had a specific set of expectations for what would be on the other side and none of them were this.

"The fuck?" someone says near my left shoulder.

I agree with the sentiment.

Most of the room moves toward the transparent wall without deciding to. Collective motion, people following people. I let it carry me forward until I'm through the glass — it gives way like a surface tension, not glass at all — and then I slow down and let the group spread out.

I keep Soo-Yun behind my left shoulder. I scan the treeline ahead. The light here is wrong for the time I remember it being when I left work — cooler, lower-angled, like late afternoon somewhere other than where I was. The undergrowth is dense enough to hide something at 5 meters. The soil is soft, dark, the kind that takes prints.

In the clearing about 20 meters out stands something I have to categorize before I react.

About 1.5 meters tall. Greenish-grey. Shell still wet enough to catch light. It's holding a machete with the easy grip of something that has used one before — weight on the back hand, blade angled slightly down, no tension in the wrist. The posture of something that has held a weapon long enough that holding it is the resting state.

I know what kappas are from mythology. This is a kappa. The shell is real — I can see the segmented edges from here, the sheen on it. Mythology didn't cover speed or intelligence or what they want with a machete.

The blade is still at its side. The posture isn't the posture of something about to swing. It's the posture of something waiting to see what we do first.

That's a data point.
